text.title: css-in-js-basics Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for css-in-js-basics. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Always prefix styled-components props with $ (e.g., $variant, $size, $color). Plain prop names leak to the DOM and trigger React unknown-prop warnings like "Received `true` for a non-boolean attribute".
rule.rationale: React forwards unknown props to the DOM element. Styled-components forwards unknown props unless prefixed with $, producing console warnings and invalid HTML attributes. This requires either the $ prefix or explicit shouldForwardProp filtering.

rule.statement: Declare a DefaultTheme augmentation in types/styled.d.ts. Without it, the theme parameter inside template literals is typed any and color values become magic strings instead of design tokens.
rule.rationale: The styled-components DefaultTheme is empty by default. Augmenting it gives TypeScript the full theme shape, enabling autocomplete and type checking inside styled blocks. This prevents agents from inlining color hex values and breaking the design token contract.

rule.statement: Pick exactly one CSS-in-JS library per app. Never mix styled-components and Emotion in the same bundle.
rule.rationale: Both runtimes are included in the bundle even if only one is actively used in the codebase. Each runtime re-injects its global style sheet, causing unpredictable cascade order and style conflicts. Pick one and stick with it across the entire application.

rule.statement: Do not define a styled.X component inside a React function body. Every render creates a new component class with a new generated className, causing re-mounts and style thrashing on every state change.
rule.rationale: Styled component definitions are effectively class definitions. When defined inside a render function, a new class is created on every render cycle. The component's key changes, triggering unmount/remount of any instances. Move styled definitions to module scope.

rule.statement: For Next.js Pages Router with styled-components, configure the Babel plugin and _document.tsx SSR registry. For App Router, wrap the tree in a 'use client' registry component, not _document.tsx.
rule.rationale: Pages Router uses _document.tsx; App Router does not. Copying Pages Router SSR setup into App Router breaks styled-components style injection. App Router requires a 'use client' registry component wrapping the entire tree at the layout level.

rule.statement: Add the babel-plugin-styled-components in dev. Display names make DevTools usable; dead-code elimination shrinks production bundle.
rule.rationale: Without the Babel plugin, styled-components show as "Styled(div)" in DevTools. With the plugin, display names become readable (e.g., "Button"). The plugin also enables dead-code elimination at build time.

rule.statement: Use the @emotion/babel-plugin for automatic jsxImportSource injection. Without it, add /** @jsxImportSource @emotion/react */ at the top of every file that uses the css prop.
rule.rationale: Without the pragma or Babel plugin, the css prop is not recognized by the JSX transform and silently falls back to a plain style attribute, producing no error but no Emotion styles either. The Babel plugin is the recommended approach to avoid per-file pragmas.

rule.statement: Define reusable style fragments as css() objects at module scope, then compose them via template literal interpolation.
rule.rationale: Composing css() objects at module scope avoids re-creating style objects on every render. Composition is idiomatic Emotion and produces stable class names.

rule.statement: For Next.js App Router with Emotion, wrap the tree in a 'use client' CacheProvider component. Emotion runtime is not compatible with React Server Components.
rule.rationale: Without CacheProvider in App Router, Emotion's runtime cannot inject styles server-side. FOUC occurs on first paint and hydration mismatches appear in the console. CacheProvider must wrap the entire app.
